title: Social Proof

content.statement: People look to others when uncertain, especially others similar to themselves.
content.question: What specific social proof would reassure a prospect most similar to my ideal customer?
content.equation: Social Proof Strength = (Visibility + Similarity + Volume + Authenticity) ÷ 4
content.business_lens: Show specific testimonials from customers similar to the prospect; counts and adoption rates matter.
content.life_lens: Notice when you are following the crowd uncritically; verify whether the crowd is actually similar to you.
sop: Collect testimonials from segments matching your prospects
Show recognizable logos or names where possible
Avoid fake or generic proof, it backfires
content.explainer: Cialdini’s studies on energy conservation showed neighbors’ actual usage was the strongest motivator, stronger than environmental, financial, or moral framing. Specific, similar, visible proof is the highest-leverage signal.
questions: What specific social proof would reassure a prospect most similar to my ideal customer?
If you removed every testimonial and number from your page, what would actually be left to convince a skeptical buyer?
Are the people in your case studies who your prospects want to become, or just whoever agreed to talk to you?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Visibility 7, Similarity 9, Volume 5, Authenticity 8. Score = (7 + 9 + 5 + 8) ÷ 4 = 7.3. Higher means prospects will trust your proof enough to lower their guard; lower means the proof is too generic, too hidden, or too thin to move anyone.
content.closing_statement: People copy people who look like them.

caption:
Principle Nº 301: Social Proof

People look to others when uncertain, especially others similar to themselves.

Ask yourself: What specific social proof would reassure a prospect most similar to my ideal customer?
